title: Asynchronous Event-Driven Architecture (GCP Pub/Sub)
description: Event-driven architecture on GCP using Pub/Sub for durable publish/subscribe messaging with push and pull subscriptions. Decouples producers from consumers; embraces eventual consistency with at-least-once delivery, configurable ack deadlines, and dead-letter topics.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: GCP Pub/Sub pricing is consumption-based. Minimum ($10-50) covers small workloads: ~10M messages/month at $0.40 per million messages, plus minimal storage for retained messages. Maximum ($5000+) represents high-throughput production systems: 1B+ messages/month ($400), extended message retention ($1000+), cross-region egress ($500+), multiple topics/subscriptions with high fan-out ($1000+), Lite subscriptions for low-latency use cases ($500+), and snapshots/replay features ($500+). Mid-range ($200-800) typical for production microservices with 100M-500M messages/month. GCP offers 10GB message delivery free tier monthly which covers ~25M messages, making very light usage nearly free.
provenance.adoptionCost: High-medium complexity pattern. Requires: (1) Architectural redesign for async thinking - 40-60 hours ($2000-3000) understanding eventual consistency, idempotency, message ordering, and failure modes; (2) Implementation effort - 20-40 hours ($1000-2000) for topic/subscription setup, dead-letter queues, retry policies, message schemas, and monitoring; (3) Testing complexity - 20-30 hours ($1000-1500) for integration testing, message flow verification, chaos testing for network failures; (4) Team training - 16-24 hours ($800-1200) on Pub/Sub semantics, best practices, debugging async systems. Lower end if team has event-driven experience; higher if migrating from synchronous architecture. AI assistants reduce implementation time by 30-40% but architectural decisions still require human expertise.
provenance.licenseCost: GCP Pub/Sub is a fully managed service with pay-as-you-go pricing model - no upfront license fees. All costs are consumption-based (included in monthly runtime costs). No proprietary SDKs or tools required; client libraries are open source. Optional commercial tools like schema registries, enterprise monitoring (Datadog, New Relic) or event mesh platforms (Solace, Confluent) would add $5000-50000/year but are not required for core pattern implementation.

provides.reasoning: Pub/Sub architecture inherently decouples producers and consumers in time - publishers don't need subscribers to be available when messages are sent, and subscribers process messages asynchronously at their own pace.

Event-driven systems with message queuing naturally handle traffic spikes by buffering messages in the broker, allowing consumers to process at their capacity without being overwhelmed or dropping requests.
The pattern explicitly embraces eventual consistency as described, where state changes propagate asynchronously through events and consumers eventually reach a consistent state.
Pub/sub patterns enable independent scaling of producers and consumers - each can scale horizontally based on their specific load without affecting the other side of the communication.
Google Cloud Pub/Sub provides the streaming/messaging platform for this pattern.
Google Cloud Pub/Sub topics serve as event sources in this pattern.

requires.reasoning: The pattern specifically uses GCP Pub/Sub as its messaging backbone, requiring pub/sub semantics (topic-based, multiple subscribers) rather than point-to-point message queuing.

Essential for event-driven systems where at-least-once delivery guarantees mean messages may be redelivered. Consumers must handle duplicate messages safely to maintain data consistency and avoid side effects.

Operational visibility is critical for debugging event flows and message processing in distributed async systems where understanding message propagation and consumer behavior is essential for troubleshooting.

supports_nfr.reason: Pattern maximum availability is 99.99% due to GCP Pub/Sub SLA and eventual consistency model.
Pattern introduces minimum p95 latency of 100ms due to async event-driven pub/sub processing overhead.
Pattern introduces minimum p99 latency of 200ms due to tail latencies in pub/sub message delivery and processing.
Pattern achieves minimum RTO of 1 minute enabled by message persistence and fast recovery.
Pattern supports maximum throughput of 360M jobs/hour based on GCP Pub/Sub scalability limits.
Event-driven architecture adds async overhead (100ms+ latency, message routing, consumer scaling). At very low throughput, simpler synchronous request-response patterns are more appropriate.
Pattern works with or without audit logging; GCP Pub/Sub supports audit logs as optional feature.
Event-driven pattern is compatible with any tenant isolation strategy via topic/subscription isolation.
Pattern is compatible with GDPR requirements through proper message handling and retention policies.
Pattern supports HIPAA compliance when using GCP Pub/Sub with appropriate BAA and encryption.
Pattern is compatible with CCPA requirements through proper data handling in event messages.
Pattern supports SOX compliance through audit trails and message durability features.
Pattern embraces eventual consistency model and does not guarantee read-your-writes semantics.
Pattern supports strict durability via GCP Pub/Sub message persistence with configurable retention.

supports_constraints.reason: This pattern variant is specifically designed for GCP Pub/Sub implementation.
Event-driven architecture is language-agnostic as GCP Pub/Sub provides client libraries for all major languages.
Async event-driven architecture using GCP Pub/Sub is platform-agnostic and supports all application types.
Event-driven architecture inherently requires asynchronous messaging via pub/sub mechanism.
Pattern supports AI inference workloads as event consumers but does not require them.
Pattern supports multi-tenancy through topic/subscription isolation strategies but works for single-tenant apps.
Pattern supports batch processing as consumers can process events individually or in batches.
Pattern works with or without distributed transactions; saga patterns can be implemented on top.
Pattern supports real-time streaming via GCP Pub/Sub but also works for non-streaming use cases.
Pattern is neutral to vector search; event consumers may or may not use vector databases.
Pattern is neutral to document stores; event consumers may use any storage backend.
Pattern is neutral to key-value stores; event consumers may use any storage backend.
Pattern is neutral to graph databases; event consumers may use any storage backend.
Pattern is neutral to time-series databases; event consumers may use any storage backend.
Pattern supports OLTP workloads as event consumers but works for any workload type.
Pattern supports OLAP workloads as event consumers but works for any workload type.

message_retention_duration.description: How long Pub/Sub retains unacknowledged messages. Longer retention increases storage costs but provides better resilience for slow consumers or system outages.

ack_deadline.description: Maximum time a subscriber has to acknowledge a message before it's redelivered. Shorter deadlines enable faster redelivery but may cause duplicate processing; longer deadlines suit slow processing workloads.

delivery_guarantee.description: Message delivery semantics. At-least-once is standard and cost-effective but requires idempotent consumers. Exactly-once (available for specific regions) prevents duplicates but has higher latency and cost.

message_ordering.description: Whether to preserve message order using ordering keys. Enabling guarantees order per key but reduces throughput and requires careful key design to avoid hotspots.

dead_letter_topic.description: Whether to configure a dead-letter topic for messages that fail processing repeatedly. Enables debugging and manual intervention but adds complexity and storage costs.

subscription_type.description: How subscribers receive messages. Pull gives consumers control over rate and is suitable for batch processing. Push delivers to HTTP endpoints with lower latency but requires publicly accessible endpoints.

retry_policy.description: Strategy for retrying failed message deliveries. Exponential backoff prevents overwhelming downstream systems. Immediate retry suits transient failures. Custom allows fine-tuned min/max backoff configuration.
